Explanation: Chest pain with sweating, exertional onset, and dyspnea is high-risk. The GP role is urgent recognition, referral, and clear handover.
PE may present with pleuritic pain and a normal chest exam. Risk context matters.
A single non-diagnostic ECG does not make concerning chest pain safe.
Inability to speak and exhaustion are red flags. GP care should focus on urgent escalation.
PE can present with dyspnea and pleuritic pain even when auscultation is normal.
Rash plus swelling and dizziness suggests a systemic allergic emergency. Escalation is needed.
Thunderclap headache is a red flag even with a normal exam.
Severe headache with visual symptoms in pregnancy/postpartum is high-risk and needs urgent assessment.
Transient neurologic deficits may still represent a dangerous event and require urgent evaluation.
Older adults can have serious abdominal/vascular disease with subtle examination findings.
Pregnancy-related emergency must be considered with abdominal/pelvic pain and bleeding.
Pain out of proportion is a red flag and should not be dismissed.
Elderly patients may present with subtle or atypical signs of serious infection.
Non-blanching rash with systemic illness is high-risk.
Diabetic foot infection with systemic features is high-risk.
Caregiver concern and poor feeding in infants are important red flags.
Drowsiness and reduced urine output are red flags.
Non-blanching rash with fever is high-risk.
Pregnancy plus abdominal pain and syncope is a red flag.
Postpartum dyspnea/chest pain is high-risk and needs urgent assessment.
Severe headache with visual symptoms in pregnancy is a red flag.
Exertional syncope can signal cardiac danger.
Palpitations before syncope are concerning for arrhythmia.
Head injury with anticoagulant use is high-risk.
Mechanism and physiology matter more than wound size.
Chest trauma plus dyspnea is concerning.
Inconsistent injury history is a safeguarding red flag.
Toxicology history is often incomplete; drowsiness is high-risk.
Intentional overdose needs urgent medical and safety assessment even if early appearance is reassuring.
Multiple household members with headache/dizziness suggests environmental exposure such as CO.
Possible STEMI/STEMI-equivalent concern is urgent.
Syncope plus dangerous ECG features should not be reassured.
Wide complex tachycardia with symptoms is high-risk.
Vomiting, abdominal pain, and abnormal breathing in diabetes are red flags.
Recurrent hypoglycemia may indicate ongoing risk, especially with long-acting agents or poor intake.
Symptoms suggesting organ danger require urgent assessment.
Postpartum hypertension symptoms can be dangerous.
Urinary retention and saddle symptoms are red flags for cauda equina concern.
Cancer history with severe back pain and weakness is high-risk.
Painful red eye with visual symptoms in contact lens users is high-risk.
Oral swelling with drooling/dysphagia can threaten the airway.
Rapid spread with pain out of proportion is a dangerous pattern.
Mucosal involvement with rash after medication is high-risk.
Sudden testicular pain is torsion until proven otherwise at GP level.
Flank pain with fever/systemic illness is high-risk.
Older patients with vomiting and reduced urine output are high-risk.
Bilious vomiting in a child is a red flag.
Hand bites can involve deep structures and need urgent assessment.
Facial burn with hoarseness suggests airway risk.
Acute decline/confusion in elderly patients may represent serious illness.
Caregiver concern can be a key clue in frail patients.
Plan, intent, and access to means indicate high risk.
Agitation with abnormal physiology may be medical or toxicologic.
A strong referral explains presentation, red flags, concern, timing, and current status.
Safety-netting must define danger signs clearly.
Clear safety-net advice and follow-up instructions should be documented.
The GP Path is recognition/referral/safety-netting, not ED management.
A clinician’s concern that a patient is not right should lower the threshold for escalation.
